title: Detecting All Possible Bridges in a Graph with Cycles

statement.markdown: Given an undirected graph of N vertices represented as an adjacency list, find and return all possible bridges in the graph. The graph may contain self-loops.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be provided in a JSON object with a `graph` key. The `graph` key will contain an adjacency list representation of the graph. Each key in the adjacency list is a vertex, and its corresponding value will be a list of its neighboring vertices.
statement.outputFormat: Return a list of all possible bridges in the graph. Each bridge should be represented as a list of two vertices that form the bridge.
statement.notes: You can assume that the graph will not contain any multiple edges between the same pair of vertices.

examples.explanation: The graph contains the following edges: (0, 1), (0, 4), (1, 2), (1, 3), (2, 3), (3, 4). The bridges are (0, 1) and (1, 3).

hints.text: The first step is to perform a depth-first search (DFS) on the graph to identify the articulation points and bridges.

editorial.title: Detecting All Possible Bridges in a Graph with Cycles
editorial.intuition: To find the bridges in the graph, we can use the concept of low values in DFS. The low value of a vertex is the smallest vertex number that can be reached through a path from the vertex such that all the vertices on the path are either descendants of the current vertex or have a lower value than the current vertex.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible solution is to use a brute force approach by checking all possible edges to see if they are bridges. However, this approach will be inefficient and will not pass the time limit.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use DFS to find all possible bridges. We can use a recursive function to traverse the graph and keep track of the low value of each vertex.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use Tarjan's algorithm to find all possible bridges in the graph.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The algorithm works by performing a DFS traversal on the graph and using a recursive function to calculate the low value of each vertex.
editorial.dryRun: The dry run will involve creating a graph with some articulation points and bridges. The algorithm will then be run on this graph to identify the bridges.
commonMistakes: Not considering the low value of a vertex
